steps.title: A pack of values that don't change

narration.default: Sometimes a few values belong as a unit and shouldn't change once set — an x-y position on a stage, the red-green-blue parts of a colour, the day-month-year of a date. Python has a special type for these: a tuple. It looks like a list, but with round brackets instead of square — and once made, you can't add, remove, or change items. Lists grow and shrink; tuples stay fixed.

steps.title: Make a colour as a tuple

narration.default:
Type:

color = (255, 100, 50)

The round brackets and commas make a tuple. Three values inside — red, green, blue — packed together as one. This is exactly what graphics libraries use to describe a colour. Notice the brackets are () round, NOT [] square. Square brackets make a list; round brackets make a tuple.

steps.title: Pull the pieces out by index

narration.default:
Type:

r = color[0]
g = color[1]
b = color[2]

The square brackets after a tuple's name reach IN — color[0] grabs the first value, color[1] the second, color[2] the third. Important: Python counts from ZERO, not one. The first thing is at position 0. This is the same indexing that works for lists and strings.

Type:

print("Red:", r)
print("Green:", g)
print("Blue:", b)

Three prints — each labelled. Now you've taken a packed-together tuple, opened it up, and shown each piece on its own.

steps.title: Run and see the colour split

narration.default: Click Run. The terminal prints Red: 255, Green: 100, Blue: 50 — the three pieces of the original colour, neatly labelled. The tuple stayed put as a whole; we just looked into it.

Lists [a, b, c] can grow with append, shrink, and reorder. Tuples (a, b, c) cannot — once made, they're fixed. Use a tuple when the values belong together and shouldn't change as a group. Use a list when you'll be adding or removing items as the program runs. Same indexing, different rules about modification.

Use them for coordinates: position = (x, y). RGB colours: (red, green, blue). Dates: (year, month, day). Anything where the count is fixed and changing one piece without the others would be wrong. Tuples also let a function hand back more than one value at a time — return (name, score) packs both into one tuple the caller can unpack.
